default_annotator.report_text:
MAZE ransomware

MAZE ransomware was initially distributed directly via exploit kits Initial deployment of Maze ransomware via exploit kits.

Mandiant observed multiple email campaigns delivering Maze ransomware Email campaigns used to distribute Maze ransomware

or

These emails used tax, invoice, and package delivery themes with document a!achments Email campaigns containing document attachments used to distribute Maze ransomware.

inline links to documents which download and execute Maze ransomware Email campaigns that contain inline links to documents that download and execute Maze ransomware

These emails originated from a compromised or spoofed account Use of compromised accounts to send phishing emails.

contained an inline link to download a Maze executable payload Email campaigns that contain inline links to documents that download and execute Maze ransomware

These emails used the subjects "Missed package delivery" and "Your AT&T wireless bill is ready to view" emails with misleading themes (e.g., "Missed package delivery" and "Your AT&T wireless bill is ready to view") to induce victims to open the messages.

were sent using a number of malicious domains with the registrant address abusereceive@hitler.rocks Use of malicious domains to send phishing emails.

A user downloaded a malicious resumethemed Microso# Word document that contained macros Downloading and opening a Word document containing malicious macros.

launched an IcedID payload, which was ultimately used to execute an instance of BEACON Macros that execute commands to launch the IcedID payload.

An actor logged into an internet-facing system via RDP Access via RDP.

The account used to grant initial access was a generic suppo$ account Using a generic account (support account) for initial access.

An actor exploited a miscon%guration on an Internet-facing system. Exploitation of a misconfiguration on an Internet-exposed system.

An actor logged into a Citrix web po$al account with a weak password. To access the Citrix web portal.

launch a Meterpreter payload on an internal system Using Meterpreter to access and control the system.

The use of legitimate credentials and broad distribution of BEACON across victim environments Use of legitimate credentials to gain access and maintain control.

an actor create their own domain account to enable la!er-stage operations Creating an account for subsequent operations

installing BEACON payloads on many servers and workstations Transferring BEACON payloads to servers and workstations.

Web shells were deployed to an internetfacing system. Deployment of web shells on an Internet-exposed system.

Intrusion operators regularly obtained and maintained access to multiple domain and local system accounts with varying permissions that were used throughout their operations Use of various local and domain system accounts.

An actor created a new domain account and added it to the domain administrators group Creating a new domain account.

MAZE intrusion operators employed Mimikatz to collect credentials to enable privilege escalation Using Mimikatz to collect credentials.

use of Bloodhound, and more manual searches for %les containing credentials Using Bloodhound to explore Active Directory configuration and identify attack paths to gain domain privileges

Mimikatz.

The actor a!empted to %nd %les with the word "password" within the environment Searching for files containing credentials (e.g., files with the word "password")

The actor a!empted to identify hosts running the KeePass password safe so#ware identify whether keepass is installed on the target devices

Actors primarily used Procdump and Mimikatz to collect credentials used to enable later stages of their intrusion. Procdump and Mimikatz are tools used to extract credentials from system memory or system files

Bloodhound and PingCastle were also used, presumably to enable a!ackers' e&o$s to understand the impacted organization's Active Directory con%guration PingCastle is used to analyze and evaluate the security of Active Directory configuration

In this case the responsible actors also a!empted to ex%ltrate collected credentials to multiple di&erent cloud %le storage services Attempts to exfiltrate sensitive data on cloud storage services

The responsible actor executed a large number of reconnaissance scripts via Cobalt Strike to collect network, host, %lesystem, and domain related information Using Cobalt Strike to gather network configuration information

The responsible actor executed a large number of reconnaissance scripts via Cobalt Strike to collect network, host, %lesystem, and domain related information Using Cobalt Strike to discover account information

The responsible actor executed a large number of reconnaissance scripts via Cobalt Strike to collect network, host, %lesystem, and domain related information Using Cobalt Strike to discover information about files and directories on compromised systems

Multiple built-in Windows commands were used to enable network, account, and host reconnaissance of the impacted environment, Using built-in Windows commands to discover network configuration information

Multiple built-in Windows commands were used to enable network, account, and host reconnaissance of the impacted environment, Using built-in Windows commands to discover account information

the actors also supplied and used Advanced IP Scanner and Ad%nd to suppo$ this stage of their operations Using Advanced IP Scanner to scan IP and network devices.

a batch script named '2.bat' which contained a series of nslookup commands Using nslookup to identify information about remote systems

FTP server Using an application layer protocol (FTP) to exfiltrate data

via an encoded PowerShell script Using PowerShell to run scripts, in this case to exfiltrate data.

via an encoded PowerShell script Obfuscated Files or Information

Bloodhound

PowerSploit/PowerView PowerSploit/PowerView (Invoke-ShareFinder) can be used to scan the network and locate available services and resources.

a reconnaissance script designed to enumerate directories across internal hosts Using reconnaissance scripts to enumerate directories on internal hosts.

An actor employed the ad%nd tool and a batch script to collect information about their network, hosts, domain, and users. Collection of network configuration information

An actor employed the ad%nd tool and a batch script to collect information about their network, hosts, domain, and users. Collection of user account information.

An actor employed the ad%nd tool and a batch script to collect information about their network, hosts, domain, and users. Collection of information on trust relationships between domains.

An actor employed the ad%nd tool and a batch script to collect information about their network, hosts, domain, and users. Using batch scripts to automate commands and gather information.

The output from this batch script (2ad%nd.bat) was saved into an archive named 'ad.7z' using an instance of the 7zip archiving utility named 7.exe. Storage and compression of locally collected data.

An actor collected directory listings from %le servers across an impacted environment The actor collected directory listings from file servers in the affected environment.

Cobalt Strike

they also tunneled RDP using the ngrok utility Using ngrok for tunneling RDP sessions through multi-hop proxies

employed tscon to hijack legitimate rdp sessions to enable both lateral movement and privilege escalation Use of tscon for hijacking legitimate RDP sessions, falling under the manipulation of remote services for lateral movement

Stolen credentials were then used to move laterally Using compromised accounts (services and users) to move laterally through the network

across the network via RDP Use of stolen credentials to move laterally through RDP

Metasploit

using a local administrator account Using local accounts with administrator privileges to move laterally and to install payloads

EternalBlue

MAZE

a base64-encoded PowerShell script Using PowerShell to execute data exfiltration commands.

a prede%ned FTP server using a hard-coded username and password Use of hard-coded credentials for FTP authentication during data exfiltration.

A di&erent base64-encoded PowerShell command Using PowerShell to execute data exfiltration commands.

Actors deploying MAZE ransomware have also used the utility WinSCP to ex%ltrate data to an a!acker-controlled FTP server Using file transfer applications such as WinSCP to exfiltrate data on an FTP server controlled by the attacker

An actor has been observed employing a %le replication utility and copying the stolen data to a cloud %le hosting/sharing service. Using file synchronization or replication applications to copy data to cloud file hosting or sharing services

threat actors employed the 7zip utility to archive data from across various corporate %le shares Using utilities such as 7zip to compress data from various corporate file shares before exfiltration.

These archives were then ex%ltrated to an a!acker-controlled server via FTP using the WinSCP utility Using file transfer applications such as WinSCP to exfiltrate data on an FTP server controlled by the attacker.

Five days a#er data was ex%ltrated from a victim environment the actor copied a MAZE ransomware binary to 15 hosts within the victim environment and successfully executed it on a po$ion of these systems. Distribution and execution of MAZE ransomware on numerous servers and workstations within the victim's environment.

A!ackers employed batch scripts and a series to txt %les containing host names to distribute and execute MAZE ransomware on many servers and workstations across the victim environment Using batch scripts to deploy and execute MAZE ransomware on many servers and workstations in the victim's environment.

using a domain administrator account created earlier in the intru Using a previously created domain administrator account to access systems and deploy MAZE ransomware.

Each of these batch scripts contained a series of commands that employed the copy command, WMIC, and PsExec to copy and execute a kill script (windows.bat) and an instance of MAZE ransomware (sss.exe) on hosts across the impacted environment Using PsExec for remote execution of scripts and ransomware on affected systems.

Notably, forensic analysis of the impacted environment revealed MAZE deployment scripts targeting ten times as many hosts as were ultimately encrypted Running MAZE ransomware (sss.exe) on different systems to encrypt data
